blocks.text: Do not be conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your minds, so that you may discern what is the will of God—what is good and acceptable and perfect.
2 : , can also be translated as “this age,” a reference to Paul's distinction between the “present evil age” (see Gal 1.4) and the new creation. , compare the state of having a “debased mind” (1.28).

For by the grace given to me I say to everyone among you not to think of yourself more highly than you ought to think, but to think with sober judgment, each according to the measure of faith that God has assigned.

3 : , might best be understood as “measure that is faith.” Our self-assessment is to be done in the context of faith; and faith is what God has (lit., “distributed”). Faith is understood here as a gift.
For as in one body we have many members, and not all the members have the same function,
so we, who are many, are one body in Christ, and individually we are members one of another.
We have gifts that differ according to the grace given to us: prophecy, in proportion to faith;
ministry, in ministering; the teacher, in teaching;
the exhorter, in exhortation; the giver, in generosity; the leader, in diligence; the compassionate, in cheerfulness.
12.1–8: The holy life.
4–8 : See also 1 Cor 12.12–30. God's gifts to particular members of the church are to be used for the body of Christ.
Let love be genuine; hate what is evil, hold fast to what is good;
love one another with mutual affection; outdo one another in showing honor.
9–10a : , Paul starts with the most excellent gift (see 1 Cor 12.31–13.1–13).
Do not lag in zeal, be ardent in spirit, serve the Lord.
Rejoice in hope, be patient in suffering, persevere in prayer.
Contribute to the needs of the saints; extend hospitality to strangers.
Bless those who persecute you; bless and do not curse them.
Rejoice with those who rejoice, weep with those who weep.
Live in harmony with one another; do not be haughty, but associate with the lowly; do not claim to be wiser than you are.
Do not repay anyone evil for evil, but take thought for what is noble in the sight of all.
If it is possible, so far as it depends on you, live peaceably with all.
Beloved, never avenge yourselves, but leave room for the wrath of God; for it is written, “Vengeance is mine, I will repay, says the Lord.”
19 : , lit., “give place to the wrath”; as the NRSV translation indicates, interpreters understand Paul to be referring to God's eschatological wrath (see 3.5). Deut. 32.35.
No, “if your enemies are hungry, feed them; if they are thirsty, give them something to drink; for by doing this you will heap burning coals on their heads.”
20 : See Prov 25.21–22. Perhaps the reference to is to burning shame for having been repaid good for evil.
Do not be overcome by evil, but overcome evil with good.
12.9–21: The character of Christian life.
